evidence_quotes.quote: preserving video games as they are not only sold as a product, but could be considered by many as a very important art form that shouldn't be at risk of disappearing while we have a very realistic chance of keeping it safe.

Publishers should not be able to have a kill switch for a game you have paid for that they can arbitrarily shut off whenever they feel like it, and there needs to be a mandatory end-of-life plan in order for the art to have a chance to be preserved.

paragraphs.text: Thank you for your time, I am interested in seeing further development when it comes to preserving video games as they are not only sold as a product, but could be considered by many as a very important art form that shouldn't be at risk of disappearing while we have a very realistic chance of keeping it safe. Publishers should not be able to have a kill switch for a game you have paid for that they can arbitrarily shut off whenever they feel like it, and there needs to be a mandatory end-of-life plan in order for the art to have a chance to be preserved. The industry has several times shown an interest in revoking as many rights from the consumers as possible over time, pick the petals off of a flower progressively until there are no petals left. As many others have mentioned, the Stop Killing Games initiative explains it better than I ever could. Even some online games from the 1990s are playable even today without the developers or publishers having to host any servers at all, thanks to the games having systems in place for the consumers themselves to upkeep the games as it is in their interest and passion. There have been several games me and my friends can no longer play but a recent one we discovered as we tried playing it is Command & Conquer: Red Alert 3, due to the publishers (EA, Electronic Arts) servers being taken down I view the aforementioned paragraphs have the most pressing issues currently, but there are many more problematic behaviors and design choices in the video game industry that keep getting worse due to the lack of regulation, and outright rewards anti-consumer behavior due to the nature of the designs utilizing for instance gambling, dark patterns (many involving micro-transactions), FOMO (Fear Of Missing Out), and unfinished games sold on promises never delivered. I also believe there needs to be online regulation on access to cheat software for online video games as it has become such a massively lucrative business that is ruining nearly every single online game for everyone else just trying to enjoy the product or service they have paid for. It is easily available for anyone to find online requiring borderline no technical skill anymore (as in, you can just Google search for it and find it instantly), as easily accessible as any other product or service you can purchase, but the product/service these cheat makers sell outright ruin the product for everyone else who is just trying to play fair and enjoy what they have purchased, while also often being involved in very shady business practices. I realize this is such a massive and unrealistic undertaking that I can't even expect developers/publishers of video games to be able to fix it themselves, so there needs to be regulation in how easy it is to find this kind of software (and even hardware) online. Even anyone using social media whose algorithm is tuned to online video games *seems* to have quite a high chance of being exposed and encouraged to acquire cheats, as just one admittedly big component in its wide spread nature. Some games I play/played affected by this are: - Battlefield 1 - Battlefield V - Counter-Strike 2 - Dark and Darker - Dark Souls 1, 2, and 3 - Elden Ring - The Finals - For Honor - Grand Theft Auto V - Halo: The Master Chief Collection - Insurgency: Sandstorm - Left 4 Dead 2 - MORDHAU - STAR WARS Battlefront 2 - Team Fortress 2 - Titanfall 2 - Tom Clancy's Rainbow Six Siege - Heroes of the Storm That said, this issue is truly so widespread that it is more likely than not present in whichever online game (especially on the PC platform, but getting more and more widespread on console platforms as well) of your choosing. Thank you, sincerely.
